# Quantum Optimal Transport — Hands-On Course

> Machine index for AI assistants. Each entry: notebook path — title — purpose.
> Generated by scripts/gen_llms_txt.py (do not hand-edit). See AGENTS.md for how to tutor.

## 00_GettingStarted
- [notebooks/00_GettingStarted/01_environment_setup.ipynb](notebooks/00_GettingStarted/01_environment_setup.ipynb) — 01 — Environment Setup — Confirm your tools are installed and run your very first quantum circuit, so everything that follows runs cleanly.
- [notebooks/00_GettingStarted/02_what_is_optimal_transport.ipynb](notebooks/00_GettingStarted/02_what_is_optimal_transport.ipynb) — 02 — What Is Optimal Transport? — Build the central idea of the whole course by hand: moving a pile of mass into a new shape at the least possible cost.
## 01_QuantumFoundations
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/01_amplitudes_and_superposition.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/01_amplitudes_and_superposition.ipynb) — 01 — Amplitudes & Superposition — Write a qubit as a pair of complex amplitudes and meet superposition — the first genuinely quantum idea.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/02_why_amplitudes_are_complex.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/02_why_amplitudes_are_complex.ipynb) — 02 — Why Amplitudes Are Complex — Answer the question `01/01` set aside: why a qubit's amplitudes are *complex* numbers, and what that hidden phase actually does.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/03_the_bloch_sphere.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/03_the_bloch_sphere.ipynb) — 03 — The Bloch Sphere — Turn every pure qubit into a single point on a sphere — the compass we navigate by for the rest of the course.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/04_measurement_and_born_rule.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/04_measurement_and_born_rule.ipynb) — 04 — Measurement & the Born Rule — Turn amplitudes into the probabilities a measurement actually returns — and discover the one thing a measurement cannot see.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/05_inner_product_and_dirac.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/05_inner_product_and_dirac.ipynb) — 05 — The Inner Product & Dirac Notation — Learn the one piece of algebra every later notebook leans on: the inner product $\langle\varphi|\psi\rangle$ that turns two states into a single number.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/06_gates_as_unitary_evolution.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/06_gates_as_unitary_evolution.ipynb) — 06 — Gates as Unitary Evolution — Meet the operations that *move* a qubit — and discover why preserving the inner product forces every one of them to be unitary.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/07_pauli_gates.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/07_pauli_gates.ipynb) — 07 — The Pauli Gates — Meet $X$, $Y$, $Z$ — the three most important single-qubit gates — and their double life as both operations *and* observables.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/08_hadamard_and_measurement_bases.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/08_hadamard_and_measurement_bases.ipynb) — 08 — Hadamard & Measurement Bases — Meet the gate every circuit opens with, and turn it around to *measure in a different basis* — the recipe that explains the `h` and `sdg` you will see in `01/11` and `01/15`.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/09_phase_and_rotation_gates.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/09_phase_and_rotation_gates.ipynb) — 09 — Phase & Rotation Gates — Pick up the dials that send a qubit to *any* point on the Bloch sphere: the phase gates $S, S^\dagger$ and the continuous rotations $R_x, R_y, R_z$.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/10_expectation_values.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/10_expectation_values.ipynb) — 10 — Expectation Values — Make precise the single number a real experiment reports — the expectation value $\langle A\rangle = \langle\psi|A|\psi\rangle$ — and build the bridge to tomography.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/11_shot_noise_and_first_circuit.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/11_shot_noise_and_first_circuit.ipynb) — 11 — Shot Noise & Your First Circuit — Close the qubit arc: sample the Born rule the way a real device does, watch the estimate converge, and run it all as a genuine Qiskit circuit.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/12_from_states_to_density_matrix.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/12_from_states_to_density_matrix.ipynb) — 12 — From States to the Density Matrix — Promote a state vector to an operator — the density matrix $\rho$ — and meet the central character of the rest of the course.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/13_pure_vs_mixed.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/13_pure_vs_mixed.ipynb) — 13 — Pure vs Mixed — Meet the maximally mixed state, set it beside $|+\rangle$, and find the single contrast the whole course is built to explain.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/14_fidelity_and_trace_distance.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/14_fidelity_and_trace_distance.ipynb) — 14 — Fidelity & Trace Distance — Pick up two honest yardsticks for "how different are two states?", and watch mixedness become a position inside the Bloch ball.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/15_tomography_of_a_noisy_qubit.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/15_tomography_of_a_noisy_qubit.ipynb) — 15 — Tomography of a Noisy Qubit — Close the density arc: reconstruct $\rho$ from real, noisy measurements and watch purity fall below $1$ — the honest reason the density matrix exists.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/16_tensor_and_partial_trace.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/16_tensor_and_partial_trace.ipynb) — 16 — Tensor Product & Partial Trace — Combine two qubits into one system, then recover a part — meeting the quantum version of a marginal.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/17_cnot_and_two_qubit_gates.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/17_cnot_and_two_qubit_gates.ipynb) — 17 — CNOT & Two-Qubit Gates — Meet the one gate that lets two qubits talk to each other — and use it to build the entangled Bell state from scratch.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/18_entanglement.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/18_entanglement.ipynb) — 18 — Entanglement — Build a two-qubit state that is perfectly definite as a whole, yet whose parts are maximally uncertain — something no classical joint distribution can do.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/19_quantum_channels.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/19_quantum_channels.ipynb) — 19 — Quantum Channels — How a quantum state *changes* — and how it picks up noise. Meet CPTP maps, watch a channel mix $|+\rangle$ toward $I/2$, and close Movement 1 of the course.
## 02_InformationAndGeometry
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/01_shannon_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/01_shannon_entropy.ipynb) — 01 — Shannon Entropy — Measure uncertainty as average surprise — the first tool of information theory, and the spine of everything that follows.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/02_kl_divergence.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/02_kl_divergence.ipynb) — 02 — KL Divergence — Measure the cost of a wrong model — the "extra bits" of using $q$ when the truth is $p$. This is the parent quantity of the entire course.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/03_mutual_information.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/03_mutual_information.ipynb) — 03 — Mutual Information — Measure how much two variables share — the information one carries about the other.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/04_transfer_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/04_transfer_entropy.ipynb) — 04 — Transfer Entropy — Close the classical-information arc: make information flow **directed** — who drives whom — and meet the honesty caveats every estimate needs.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/05_the_probability_simplex.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/05_the_probability_simplex.ipynb) — 05 — The Probability Simplex — See the space of *all* categorical distributions as a single geometric object — a triangle — and ask the question that opens the geometry arc: what is the natural way to measure distance on it?
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/06_fisher_rao_metric.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/06_fisher_rao_metric.ipynb) — 06 — The Fisher–Rao Metric — Discover that the simplex is curved: the natural metric of statistical distinguishability blows up at the corners, so the boundary is infinitely far away.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/07_fisher_rao_geodesics.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/07_fisher_rao_geodesics.ipynb) — 07 — Fisher–Rao Geodesics — Compute the geodesic distance on the curved simplex and draw the shortest paths — great-circle arcs that bow away from the boundary.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/08_two_geometries_one_simplex.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/08_two_geometries_one_simplex.ipynb) — 08 — Two Geometries on One Simplex — Close the geometry arc with the punchline that powers the rest of the course: the same simplex carries *two* distinct geometries — information and transport — and they give different answers to "what is the halfway distribution?"
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/09_umegaki_relative_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/09_umegaki_relative_entropy.ipynb) — 09 — Umegaki Relative Entropy — Lift the KL divergence to density matrices — and watch it see the coherence a classical KL is blind to.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/10_quantum_mutual_information.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/10_quantum_mutual_information.ipynb) — 10 — Quantum Mutual Information — Lift mutual information to density matrices — and find a Bell pair shares twice what any classical pair can.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/11_negative_conditional_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/11_negative_conditional_entropy.ipynb) — 11 — Negative Conditional Entropy — Meet the sharpest signature of entanglement: a conditional entropy that drops below zero — impossible for any classical pair.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/12_bures_distance.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/12_bures_distance.ipynb) — 12 — Bures Distance — Close Movement 2: lift the Fisher–Rao distance to density matrices, and complete the classical-to-quantum information dictionary.
## 03_ClassicalOptimalTransport
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/01_the_monge_problem.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/01_the_monge_problem.ipynb) — 01 — The Monge Problem — Ask optimal transport's founding question — *what is the cheapest way to reshape one pile of mass into another?* — and meet Monge's original answer: a deterministic map.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/02_where_monge_breaks.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/02_where_monge_breaks.ipynb) — 02 — Where Monge Breaks — Meet the obstruction that defeats Monge's deterministic map: when one pile of mass must *split* to fill another, no function can describe the move.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/03_the_kantorovich_lp.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/03_the_kantorovich_lp.ipynb) — 03 — The Kantorovich LP — Relax Monge's map into a *coupling* that may split mass, and optimal transport becomes a linear program that always has a solution.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/04_birkhoff_and_assignment.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/04_birkhoff_and_assignment.ipynb) — 04 — Birkhoff and the Assignment Problem — Close the Monge→Kantorovich arc with its most elegant special case — uniform marginals, where transport becomes the classical assignment problem — and open the transport row of the classical↔quantum dictionary.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/05_wasserstein_distance.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/05_wasserstein_distance.ipynb) — 05 — The Wasserstein Distance — Promote the Kantorovich *cost* into a genuine *distance* — one that, unlike the KL divergence, listens to the geometry of the ground space.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/06_1d_quantile_closed_form.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/06_1d_quantile_closed_form.ipynb) — 06 — The 1-D Closed Form — On the line, the whole linear program collapses to *sort and match by quantile* — and $W_1$ becomes the plain area between two cumulative curves.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/07_mccann_geodesic.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/07_mccann_geodesic.ipynb) — 07 — The McCann Geodesic — Close the Wasserstein arc with its most beautiful feature: the $W_2$ geodesic *slides* mass through the ground space — the transport-geometry half of the two-geometries hinge.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/08_kantorovich_duality.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/08_kantorovich_duality.ipynb) — 08 — Kantorovich Duality — Every transport LP has a hidden twin: a *dual* that prices positions instead of moving mass. The two views give the same number — and the dual is where the deep structure lives.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/09_entropic_regularization.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/09_entropic_regularization.ipynb) — 09 — Entropic Regularization — Reward the transport plan a little for being *spread out*, and the problem turns smooth, strongly convex, and fast. At its heart sits one object: the Gibbs kernel.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/10_sinkhorn_algorithm.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/10_sinkhorn_algorithm.ipynb) — 10 — The Sinkhorn Algorithm — Bend the Gibbs kernel onto the marginal constraints by nothing more than rescaling rows and columns in turn. Five lines, two matrix products per step — the algorithm that made optimal transport fast.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/11_amari_bridge.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/11_amari_bridge.ipynb) — 11 — Amari's Bridge — The identity the whole course has been walking toward: the Sinkhorn plan is the **KL projection** of the Gibbs kernel onto the transportation polytope. Transport geometry *is* information geometry.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/12_bures_wasserstein.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/12_bures_wasserstein.ipynb) — 12 — Bures–Wasserstein — For one special family — the Gaussians — optimal transport needs no algorithm at all. $W_2$ is a closed-form expression in the means and covariances.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/13_the_bures_bridge.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/13_the_bures_bridge.ipynb) — 13 — The Bures Bridge — Watch one formula change identity. The matrix term of the Gaussian Wasserstein distance, fed density matrices instead of covariances, *becomes* the quantum Bures distance of `02/12`. This is the door into quantum optimal transport.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/14_benamou_brenier_otto.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/14_benamou_brenier_otto.ipynb) — 14 — Benamou–Brenier and Otto — Close Module 3 by viewing the $W_2$ geodesic two ways — McCann's explicit affine map, and Benamou–Brenier's least-energy flow — and complete the dictionary that carries transport into the quantum world.
## 04_QuantumOptimalTransport
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/01_diagonal_collapse.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/01_diagonal_collapse.ipynb) — 01 — The Diagonal-Collapse Problem — Open quantum optimal transport with the question that makes it necessary: *if we reduce a quantum state to the probabilities a measurement returns, what do we throw away?*
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/02_noncommuting_same_diagonal.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/02_noncommuting_same_diagonal.ipynb) — 02 — Non-Commuting States, Same Diagonal — In `01_diagonal_collapse` you saw two *different* states share a $Z$-diagonal; here you meet a sharper version of the same trap — two states that do not even commute, yet still collapse onto the identical diagonal.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/03_trevisan_taxonomy.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/03_trevisan_taxonomy.ipynb) — 03 — Trevisan's Taxonomy and the Consistency Principle — Synthesise the opening arc of Module 4: turn the two examples you built into a *law* every quantum optimal transport must obey, then place the competing definitions of QOT on one map.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/04_quantum_coupling_sdp.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/04_quantum_coupling_sdp.ipynb) — 04 — The Quantum Coupling SDP — Build the object the taxonomy named: a *quantum coupling* of two states, optimised by a semidefinite program — the operator-level lift of the Kantorovich LP.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/05_solving_qot_in_cvxpy.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/05_solving_qot_in_cvxpy.ipynb) — 05 — Solving Quantum OT in cvxpy — Point the coupling SDP at the example the module has carried since the start — separate $|+\rangle\langle+|$ from $I/2$, read the optimal coupling, and close the transport row of the running dictionary.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/06_entropic_qot_gibbs.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/06_entropic_qot_gibbs.ipynb) — 06 — Entropic Quantum OT and the Gibbs Kernel — Smooth the coupling SDP the way module 03 smoothed the Kantorovich LP — add a von Neumann entropy bonus $-\varepsilon\,S(\rho_{AB})$ — and watch the **matrix-exponential Gibbs kernel** $K = \exp(-C/\varepsilon)$ appear at the centre of the regularised problem.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/07_operator_sinkhorn_iteration.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/07_operator_sinkhorn_iteration.ipynb) — 07 — The Operator Sinkhorn Iteration — In `04/06` a black-box convex solver handed us the entropic quantum optimal-transport plan. Here we build the explicit **operator Sinkhorn iteration** that *reaches* that same optimum on its own — alternating positive-operator rescalings of the Gibbs kernel, the matrix lift of the row/column rescaling that made classical transport fast in `03/10`.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/08_quantum_amari_bridge.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/08_quantum_amari_bridge.ipynb) — 08 — The Quantum Amari Bridge — The single identity the whole quantum arc has been walking toward: the entropic quantum optimal-transport plan is the **Umegaki relative-entropy projection** of the matrix-exponential Gibbs kernel $K = \exp(-C/\varepsilon)$ onto the set of couplings. The operator lift of `03/11`'s classical Amari identity — transport geometry *is* information geometry, now for density matrices.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/09_the_qot_zoo.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/09_the_qot_zoo.ipynb) — 09 — The QOT Zoo — Put all three quantum optimal-transport objects on one bench and ask the question the module has been circling: when do they give the *same* number, and when do they part ways? The honest answer is the whole point — they are a **reconciled family**, agreeing exactly in some regimes, ordered identically in others, and genuinely distinct in the rest.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/10_embedding_signals_into_states.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/10_embedding_signals_into_states.ipynb) — 10 — Embedding signals into states — To *do* quantum optimal transport on a signal, you first face a modelling choice that decides everything downstream: **how does a signal become a quantum state?** This notebook lays out the embedding menu and, for each option, names exactly what structure of the signal it keeps — and what it throws away.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/11_kuramoto_dyad_and_ground_truth.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/11_kuramoto_dyad_and_ground_truth.ipynb) — 11 — The Kuramoto dyad and the ground truth — The capstone begins here. Before we can ask whether a *quantum* coupling measure earns its keep, we need a problem where we already know the answer — a synthetic system whose coupling we *injected ourselves*, so "did the measure recover it?" becomes a question with a checkable answer. This notebook builds that test bed: two coupled **Kuramoto oscillators** with a known coupling strength $K$, and the two **classical baselines** every later measure must beat.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/12_naive_phase_embedding_tracks_K.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/12_naive_phase_embedding_tracks_K.ipynb) — 12 — The naive phase embedding tracks $K$ — You hold a test bed with a known answer and two classical yardsticks. Now we make the first move of the capstone proper: carry the dyad's phases across the bridge from `10`, turn each phase into a **quantum state**, and ask the question the whole module is building toward — does a *quantum* coupling measure read the injected coupling $K$ as faithfully as PLV does?
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/13_the_redundancy_reveal.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/13_the_redundancy_reveal.ipynb) — 13 — The redundancy reveal — Notebook `12` ended on a win and a question. The quantum measures rose with the injected coupling $K$ exactly as the classical baselines did — *it looked like it works*. But "rises with $K$" is the bar `11` set, and PLV already clears it. So here we ask the sharper question head-on: **does the quantum measure read anything PLV does not?** The answer we find is a real, clean result — and it points us straight at the fix.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/14_richer_embedding_applied.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/14_richer_embedding_applied.ipynb) — 14 — The richer embedding, applied — Notebook `13` left us with a sharp, structural diagnosis: on the **naive phase qubit**, the joint state $\rho_{AB}$ has a single coupling-dependent number in it, and that number is the phase-locking value. Feed a quantum measure a state that only knows PLV and it can only report PLV. The bottleneck was never quantum optimal transport — it was the embedding. This notebook builds the fix.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/15_discriminating_experiment.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/15_discriminating_experiment.ipynb) — 15 — The discriminating experiment — This is the moment the whole course has been building toward. In `14` you opened a second channel in the joint state $\rho_{AB}$ — the second circular moment of the phase difference, a quantity the phase-locking value cannot see. Today you turn that structural opening into a *measured* advantage: you build two systems that PLV is physically unable to tell apart, and watch a quantum-optimal-transport coupling measure separate them anyway.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/16_capstone_synthesis.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/16_capstone_synthesis.ipynb) — 16 — The capstone synthesis — Close the capstone by holding two truths at once: across the last notebook you *demonstrated*, with a control, that a quantum-optimal-transport coupling measure can beat the phase-locking value — and here you give that result its honest accounting, the full reckoning of when it helps, at what cost, and where its real value lies.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/17_embedding_design_amplifies_the_effect.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/17_embedding_design_amplifies_the_effect.ipynb) — 17 — Embedding design amplifies the effect — The capstone's win over the phase-locking value was real but *small* — about $0.004$ nats, a hair below the resolution of today's quantum hardware. This notebook turns a dial the capstone left at its default and shows the effect can be made **far larger and far more significant**, purely by choosing the embedding more deliberately. The advantage stays *quantum-inspired* — a better-organised classical analysis — but it becomes comfortably measurable.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/18_what_no_classical_data_embedding_can_buy.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/18_what_no_classical_data_embedding_can_buy.ipynb) — 18 — What no classical-data embedding can buy — We made the effect large in `17`. Now we ask the sharpest question of the whole capstone: is there *any* embedding — even one that **entangles** the two systems — that lets a quantum-optimal-transport measure detect coupling structure **no** classical statistic can? The honest answer, demonstrated here rather than asserted, is **no** — and seeing *why* turns the capstone's caveat into a precise structural boundary, and tells us exactly where a genuinely quantum advantage *would* have to come from.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/19_vqe_limitation_de_palma.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/19_vqe_limitation_de_palma.ipynb) — 19 — The De Palma limitation: when quantum optimal transport bounds the algorithm — Turn the geometry around. Across this module you used quantum optimal transport to *measure* — to read the coupling between two systems off a density matrix. Here you meet a result where the very same geometry becomes a *proof technique*: the quantum Wasserstein distance lower-bounds what shallow variational quantum algorithms can achieve, drawing a provable line around a whole family of near-term quantum methods.
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/20_frontier_synthesis.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/20_frontier_synthesis.ipynb) — 20 — Frontier and synthesis: the whole course in one frame — The last notebook. Bring the entire journey back into a single view — the five modules in one line each, every numerical promise the course made replayed live, the headline result that the restructure was written to deliver, and the classical ↔ quantum dictionary read in its completed form. This is a celebration with the receipts laid out beside it.
## 05_RealQuantumHardware
- [notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/01_using_a_real_quantum_computer.ipynb](notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/01_using_a_real_quantum_computer.ipynb) — 01 — Using a Real Quantum Computer — This notebook hands you the keys to an actual IBM quantum processor: you take a quantity you have so far computed *exactly* on the simulator and re-run it, unchanged, on real hardware.
- [notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/02_born_rule_on_real_hardware.ipynb](notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/02_born_rule_on_real_hardware.ipynb) — 02 — The Born Rule on Real Hardware — In `01/04–04` you sampled the Born rule on a simulator; now you read it straight off a real QPU and meet the device's read-out bias.
- [notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/03_tomography_and_fidelity_on_real_hardware.ipynb](notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/03_tomography_and_fidelity_on_real_hardware.ipynb) — 03 — Tomography & Fidelity on Real Hardware — Counts hand you probabilities — one number per outcome. But the device prepared a *whole* state, with phases and coherences that a single histogram cannot see. To recover all of it you measure in three complementary bases and reconstruct the state, then ask the honest question: how far did it fall from the ideal you asked for?
- [notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/04_quantum_mutual_information_on_real_hardware.ipynb](notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/04_quantum_mutual_information_on_real_hardware.ipynb) — 04 — Quantum Mutual Information on Real Hardware — In `02/10` a Bell pair shared exactly 2 bits of mutual information — double the classical maximum any pair of bits can reach. That factor of two *is* entanglement, written in the language of information. Here you measure that same quantity on a real superconducting chip and watch decoherence quietly steal a piece of it.
- [notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/05_geometry_on_real_hardware_bures.ipynb](notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/05_geometry_on_real_hardware_bures.ipynb) — 05 — Geometry on Real Hardware: the Bures Distance — How far did the device's state fall from the ideal you asked for? In `05/03` you answered with a fidelity below 1 — an honest score, but a *number*, not yet a *distance*. Here you turn it into one. The gap becomes a single point on the Bures geometry you built in `02/12` — the quantum Fisher–Rao distance — and then you watch that point travel outward as you add circuit depth, decoherence pushing the device's state steadily further from the ideal.
- [notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/06_capstone_on_real_hardware_and_frontier.ipynb](notebooks/05_RealQuantumHardware/06_capstone_on_real_hardware_and_frontier.ipynb) — 06 — The Capstone on Real Hardware, and the Frontier — In `04/15` you proved something delicate and real: in *simulation*, a richer multi-frequency quantum embedding's coupling measure separates two matched-PLV ensembles by about **0.004 nats** of mutual information — a structure that the phase-locking value, by construction, cannot see. That result stands. Here we ask the honest hardware question that closes the course: can a real quantum processor even *see* an effect that small? Not "does the effect exist" — it does, you measured it — but "is today's hardware sharp enough to resolve it?" We will answer with numbers, and the answer will teach us exactly where the frontier lies.
